paper_title: From Sparse to Dense: Multi-View GRPO for Flow Models via Augmented Condition Space

sections.title: Abstract
sections.content: Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as a powerful framework for preference alignment in text-to-image (T2I) flow models. However, we observe that the standard paradigm where evaluating a group of generated samples against a single condition suffers from insufficient exploration of inter-sample relationships, constraining both alignment efficacy and performance ceilings. To address this sparse single-view evaluation scheme, we propose Multi-View GRPO (MV-GRPO), a novel approach that enhances relationship exploration by augmenting the condition space to create a dense multi-view reward mapping. Specifically, for a group of samples generated from one prompt, MV-GRPO leverages a flexible Condition Enhancer to generate semantically adjacent yet diverse captions. These captions enable multi-view advantage re-estimation, capturing diverse semantic attributes and providing richer optimization signals. By deriving the probability distribution of the original samples conditioned on these new captions, we can incorporate them into the training process without costly sample regeneration. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MV-GRPO achieves superior alignment performance over state-of-the-art methods.

sections.title: Start
sections.content: 6 2 0 2 3 1 ] . [ 1 8 4 6 2 1 . 3 0 6 2 : r From Sparse to Dense: Multi-View GRPO for Flow Models via Augmented Condition Space Jiazi Bu1,2,6, Pengyang Ling3, Yujie Zhou1,6, Yibin Wang4,9, Yuhang Zang6, Tianyi Wei2, Xiaohang Zhan7, Jiaqi Wang9, Tong Wu8, Xingang Pan2 and Dahua Lin5,6,10 1Shanghai Jiao Tong University 2S-Lab, Nanyang Technological University 3University of Science and Technology of China 4Fudan University 5The Chinese University of Hong Kong 6Shanghai AI Laboratory 7Adobe Research 8Stanford University 9Shanghai Innovation Institute 10CPII under InnoHK Project Page: https://bujiazi.github.io/mvgrpo.github.io/ Fig. 1: Gallery of MV-GRPO. Our MV-GRPO substantially elevates the generation quality of flow models (Flux.1-dev in this figure), particularly in terms of fine-grained details and photorealism. Prompts are listed in the supplementary material. Abstract. Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) has emerged as powerful framework for preference alignment in text-to-image (T2I) Equal contribution. Corresponding author. 2 J. Bu et al. flow models. However, we have observed that the standard paradigm that evaluates group of generated samples against single condition suffers from insufficient exploration of inter-sample relationships, constraining both alignment efficacy and performance ceilings. To address this sparse single-view evaluation scheme, we propose Multi-View GRPO (MV-GRPO), novel approach that enhances relationship exploration by augmenting the condition space to create dense multi-view reward mapping. Specifically, for group of samples generated from one prompt, MV-GRPO leverages flexible Condition Enhancer to generate semantically adjacent yet diverse captions. These captions enable multi-view advantage re-estimation, capturing diverse semantic attributes and providing richer optimization signals. By deriving the probability distribution of the original samples conditioned on these new captions, they can be incorporated into the training process without costly sample regeneration. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MV-GRPO achieves superior alignment performance over state-of-the-art methods. Keywords: Reinforcement Learning GRPO Diffusion/Flow Model

sections.title: Introduction
sections.content: Over the past few years, diffusion/flow models [12, 20, 23, 32] have emerged as the dominant paradigm in the generative scheme, demonstrating unprecedented capability in synthesizing high-fidelity visual content [8, 14, 25, 28]. While pretraining on massive datasets [5, 22, 29] endows these models with impressive generative versatility, ensuring their outputs align with human preferences and task-specific downstream constraints still poses critical ongoing challenge [6]. Recent advances in Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based post-training paradigms [2,9,27,30] have demonstrated considerable efficacy in bridging this gap. Through optimization anchored in reward models [21, 37, 39, 41] that faithfully reflect human preferences, these methods effectively align model outputs with desired behaviors and task constraints. Among these advancements, Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [31] has stood out for its efficiency and stability. Initially grounded in Large Language Models (LLMs), GRPO estimates the advantage of each sample relative to group average under given condition (e.g., textual prompt), thereby eliminating the need for complex value network and fostering scalable, flexible framework for preference alignment. line of research [11, 19, 38, 43] have adapted GRPO for visual generation by substituting the standard ODE solvers with SDEs to introduce stochasticity during the flow sampling process. As reward estimation relies on noise-free samples generated via computationally expensive iterative denoising, it is essential to fully exploit the relationships among these hard-earned samples for preference alignment. However, existing methods typically operate under Single-View paradigm: they evaluate the generated group solely against the single initial condition. This reward evaluation protocol can be reinterpreted as sparse, one-to-many mapping from Multi-View GRPO 3 Fig. 2: Reward Evaluation in GRPO Training. (a) Standard flow-based GRPO methods evaluate generated samples under the single original condition, resulting in sparse reward mapping and insufficient inter-sample relationship exploration. (b) Our MV-GRPO leverages an augmented set of conditions to facilitate dense multi-view mapping, fostering comprehensive exploration of relationship among samples. the condition space to the data space , as shown in Fig. 2 (a). Fundamentally, this paradigm models intra-group relationships by ranking samples based on their alignment with singular condition, ignoring the multifaceted nature of visual semantics. For instance, as illustrated in Fig. 3, given SDE sample describing cat and dog within teacup, it may rank poorly under one condition (A cat and dog in teacup.) but highly under another similar condition specifying visual attributes like lighting, motion or composition. Consequently, relying solely on the ranking derived from single prompt is insufficient to gauge the nuanced relationships among samples, resulting in an inherently sparse reward mapping. In contrast, by incorporating the diverse rankings induced by novel prompts, we can effectively densify the condition-data reward signal. This strategy serves dual purposes: (i) enabling more comprehensive exploration of intra-group relationships from multiple perspectives, and (ii) establishing intrinsic contrasts by identifying ranking shifts across different conditions, thereby facilitating preference-aligned generation under various conditions. In light of the above analysis, we propose Multi-View GRPO (MV-GRPO), novel reinforcement learning framework that provides dense supervision paradigm via an Augmented Condition Space. Specifically, MV-GRPO introduces flexible Condition Enhancer module to sample cluster of semantically adjacent descriptors around the original condition anchor. As depicted in Fig. 2 (b), these augmented descriptors, along with the original condition, form multi-view condition cluster used to jointly evaluate the relative advantage relationships among the generated samples. This design offers two key benefits: (i) the multi-view evaluation paradigm reinforces the thoroughness of intra-group sample assessment and inherently facilitates the models capacity to learn ranking variations under diverse perspectives, promoting heightened awareness of conditional perturbations for enhanced preference alignment, and (ii) by augmenting the condition space rather than the computationally expensive data space , we incur only modest overhead by reusing the hard-earned noise-free samples. Extensive experiments demonstrate that MV-GRPO significantly outperforms standard single-view baselines, achieving superior visual quality and generalization capabilities. Our contribution can be summarized as follows: 4 J. Bu et al. 1. Dense Multi-View Mapping: We identify the sparsity of the single-view reward evaluation in flow-based GRPO and propose dense, multi-view supervision paradigm via augmenting the condition space. 2. MV-GRPO: We present MV-GRPO, novel GRPO framework that leverages flexible Condition Enhancer to construct an augmented condition set. By re-evaluating the probabilities of original samples under these new conditions, we enable multi-view optimization without costly regeneration. 3. Superior Performance: MV-GRPO achieves superior performance over existing baselines, excelling in both in-domain and out-of-domain evaluation.

sections.title: 2.1 Diffusion and Flow Matching
sections.content: Diffusion models [7, 12, 32, 33] have achieved exceptional performance in generative modeling by learning to reverse gradual noising process, enabling highfidelity visual synthesis across various modalities [3, 4, 10, 45]. The introduction of Latent Diffusion Models (LDMs) [28] further reduces the computational cost by performing the diffusion process in compressed latent space. Instead of simulating stochastic diffusion path, flow models [8, 18, 20] directly learn continuous-time velocity field that moves along straight lines between the noise and data distributions, offering better stability and scalability, and giving rise to numerous state-of-the-art generative models like Flux series [14, 15], QwenImage [40], HunyuanVideo series [13, 34] and WAN series [36]. 2.2 Alignment for Diffusion and Flow Models Aligning Diffusion and Flow models with human preferences has evolved from early PPO-style policy gradients [2, 30, 42] and DPO variants [24, 27, 35] toward more efficient online reinforcement learning frameworks like Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) [31]. To enable GRPO to Flow Matching, foundational works such as Flow-GRPO [19] and DanceGRPO [43] reformulate deterministic Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) sampling into equivalent Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) trajectories, facilitating the stochastic exploration necessary for policy optimization while preserving marginal probability distributions. Building upon this, several variants have emerged to refine the alignment process: TempFlow-GRPO [11] and Granular-GRPO [47] introduce dense credit assignment for precise T2I alignment. Then, efficiency is further addressed by MixGRPO [16] through hybrid ODE-SDE sampling mechanism and by BranchGRPO [17] via structured branching rollouts. DiffusionNFT [46] optimizes the forward process directly via flow matching, defining an implicit policy direction by contrasting positive and negative generations. Despite these advancements, existing frameworks typically follow sparse, one-to-many reward evaluation paradigm, leading to insufficient and suboptimal exploration. In this work, we enable dense condition-data reward mapping through efficiently augmenting the condition space, achieving more comprehensive advantage estimation and improved alignment performance. Multi-View GRPO

sections.title: 3.1 Preliminary: Flow-based GRPO
sections.content: Flow Matching as MDP. Flow-based GRPO [19,43] formulates the generation process as multi-step Markov Decision Process (MDP). Let be the condition. The agent pθ, parameterized by θ, facilitates reverse-time generation trajectory Γ = (sT , aT , . . . , s0, a0). Here, the state st = (c, t, xt) encompasses the current noisy latent xt at timestep t, initializing from xT (0, I) and terminating at the clean sample x0. The action at corresponds to the single-step denoising update derived from the policy πθ(xt1xt, c). Sampling with SDE. Standard flow matching models [8, 14] typically utilize deterministic Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) for sampling: dxt = vθ(xt, t, c)dt, (1) where vθ(xt, t, c) is the predicted flow velocity. To satisfy the stochastic exploration requirements of GRPO, prior works [19, 43] substitute the ODE with Stochastic Differential Equation (SDE) that preserves the marginal distribution: (cid:18) dxt = vθ(xt, t, c) + σ2 2t (xt + (1 t)vθ(xt, t, c)) dt + σtdwt, (2) (cid:19) (cid:113) where dwt represents the Wiener process increments. The term σt = η 1t modulates the magnitude of injected noise, governed by the hyperparameter η. For practical implementation, this is discretized via the Euler-Maruyama scheme: (cid:18) xt+t = xt + vθ(xt, t, c) + σ2 2t (xt + (1 t)vθ(xt, t, c)) + σt (cid:19) tϵ, (3) where ϵ (0, I) denotes the Gaussian noise for stochastic exploration. Training of GRPO. Given condition c, generation rollout produces set of outputs {xi 0 is then derived by comparing its reward value R(xi of xi 0, c) against the aggregate group statistics as follows: i=1. The relative advantage Ai 0}G Ai = R(xi 0, c) mean({R(xj 0, c)}G std({R(xj 0, c)}G j=1) j=1) . (cid:1) , (5) (6) (7) The coefficient β in Eq. 5 balances the KL regularization during training. 6 J. Bu et al. Fig. 3: Reward Ranking Varies with Conditions. Reward rankings of SDE samples across multiple semantically similar yet different conditions exhibit large variations, indicating that relying on single condition for advantage estimation is inadequate. 3.2 Observation and Analysis As shown in Fig. 3, given prompt condition, set of images can be generated by introducing SDE-based stochasticity into the sampling process. Although these images are consistent with the original prompt in terms of subject content, they also display certain variations, particularly in attributes or local details not specified in the original prompt. Consequently, when evaluating them with the original prompt solely through single-view paradigm, the influence of such content variations cannot be sufficiently assessed. Notably, when the prompt is perturbed (Condition 1, 2 and 3 in Fig. 3), the relative merits of these images also change accordingly. Intuitively, it is reasonable to perturb the prompt and evaluate the corresponding advantages from the novel perspectives provided by these perturbed prompts, thereby facilitating: (i) more comprehensive evaluation from diverse viewpoints, and (ii) intrinsic contrastive guidance that teaches the model how advantages shift under different prompt perturbations, thus enhancing its perceptual sensitivity to prompt variations. 3.3 Condition Enhancer To facilitate comprehensive evaluation of visual samples, we consider sampling auxiliary descriptors from the local manifold surrounding the anchor condition in the condition space for dense multi-view assessment. We formalize the Condition Enhancer operator as : 2C, which maps an anchor condition and sample group XG = {xi i=1 to an augmented condition set: 0}G VK = E(c, XG) = {c pE (c, XG)} , (8) in which VK denotes the resulting augmented condition set containing additional views, and pE represents the sampling distribution of given and XG. In practice, we provide two implementations of E: Multi-View GRPO 7 Fig. 4: Overview of MV-GRPO. MV-GRPO leverages flexible Condition Enhancer module (a pretrained VLM or LLM) to generate diverse augmented conditions for dense multi-view reward signals, facilitating comprehensive advantage estimation. cpost 0, PVLM), = 1 . . . K(cid:9) , Online VLM Enhancer. To dynamically capture the visual semantics of generated samples, pretrained Vision-Language Model (VLM) is employed as an online Condition Enhancer EVLM. During the training loop, EVLM projects each sample xi 0 XG back to the condition space to obtain posterior descriptors: = (cid:8)cpost post pEVLM (c, xi where the prompt PVLM instructs EVLM to describe visual contents within xi 0. For each enhancement given by Eq. 9, PVLM is randomly sampled from an instruction set PVLM covering diverse descriptive perspectives (e.g., lighting, composition, style, etc.). The above design guarantees the diversity of augmented conditions from two aspects: (i) First, each cpost is derived from unique SDE sample xi 0 XG; (ii) Second, EVLM is queried with varied instructions PVLM PVLM focusing on different attributes. In the implementation, we set = to fully leverage the generated samples within XG. Offline LLM Enhancer. As complementary strategy based purely on textual semantics, pretrained Large Language Model (LLM) is utilized as an offline Condition Enhancer ELLM, which directly samples prior descriptors given the anchor condition c: (9) V prior = (cid:110) cprior cprior pELLM(c, Mem, PLLM), = 1 . . . (cid:111) , (10) where the prompt PLLM instructs ELLM to rewrite the condition. Mirroring the online mode to ensure diversity, (i) Mem represents historical output buffer introduced to prevent duplicate responses, and (ii) PLLM is randomly chosen from an editing prompt set PLLM, which includes three operations: addition, deletion, and rewriting. Crucially, since ELLM operates independently of image generation, it can be executed entirely offline before training. The full details of all VLM and LLM prompts are provided in the supplementary material. 3.4 Multi-View GRPO Building upon the expanded prompts generated through condition enhancement and their associated condition-data mappings, we develop MV-GRPO, multiview flow-based GRPO framework that densely couples generated samples with diverse conditions. The overview of MV-GRPO is illustrated in Fig. 4. 8 J. Bu et al. The model is fine-tuned on mixed set of both the original condition and the augmented conditions. The final MV-GRPO objective is constructed by aggregating the policy gradient losses across the anchor view and the augmented conditions in VK, with the KL term omitted for brevity: JMV-GRPO(θ) = (cid:34) 1 (cid:88) (cid:88) i=1 1 1 (cid:124) cC,{xi 0}G i=1πθold (c),{ck}K k=1pE (c,XG) min (cid:16) t(θ)Ai,c ri , clip(ri t(θ), 1 ε, 1 + ε)Ai,c t=0 (cid:123)(cid:122) Objective for the Original Condition + (cid:17) (cid:125) 1 (cid:88) i= 1 1 (cid:88) t=0 (cid:88) k=1 (cid:124) min (cid:16) (θ, ck)Ai,ck , clip(r (θ, ck), 1 ε, 1 + ε)Ai,ck (cid:123)(cid:122) Objective for Augmented Conditions (11) (cid:17) (cid:35) , (cid:125) is the advantage for the sample xi where Ai,ck (derived from Eq. 4 by substituting with ck), with ri the importance sampling ratios conditioned on and ck, respectively: 0 under an augmented condition ck (θ, ck) denoting t(θ) and ri t(θ) = pθ(xi pθold(xi t1xi t1xi t, c) t, c) , (θ, ck) = pθ(xi pθold (xi t1xi t1xi t, ck) t, ck) . (12) Multi-View GRPO 9 Fig. 5: Distribution of Probability Drift at Different SDE Steps. Most condition pairs exhibit drift near zero, demonstrating that the SDE transition probability is effectively preserved when substituting the original with augmented conditions. The training pipeline of MV-GRPO is detailed in Algorithm 1. Theoretical Perspective. To justify optimizing the policy conditioned on an augmented view ck using trajectories generated under the anchor c, we examine the transition probability dynamics. Recall from Eq. 3 that the single-step transition from xt to xt1 (denoted as step size t) follows Gaussian distribution. The transition mean µθ and covariance Σt derived from the SDE solver are given by: µθ(xt, c) = xt + vθ(xt, t, c) + (cid:18) σ2 2t (xt + (1 t)vθ(xt, t, c)) t, (13) (cid:19) Σt = σ2 tI. (14) Consequently, the policy πθ(xt1xt, c) can be modeled as (xt1; µθ(xt, c), Σt), where the probability density is formulated as: pθ(xt1xt, c) = 1 (cid:112)(2π)dΣt (cid:18) exp 1 xt1 µθ(xt, c)2 Σ1 (cid:19) . (15) When evaluating this transition under new augmented condition ck VK, the sampled point xt1 (which was generated via c) is fixed. The probability density of observing this specific transition under the new view ck is given by: (cid:19) (cid:18) 1 (cid:112)(2π)dΣt 1 2 exp xt1 µθ(xt, ck) Σ1 . (16) pθ(xt1xt, ck) = The probability drift induced by the condition perturbation is defined as the absolute difference in log-probability densities: δ(c, ck) = log pθ(xt1xt, c) log pθ(xt1xt, ck) . (17) We sampled 500 pairs of (c, ck) through the VLM enhancer and calculate their corresponding probability drift, with the resulting distribution plotted in Fig. 5. Specifically, it can be observed that the drift is minimal for the vast majority of cases across different SDE steps, which is ensured by our Condition Enhancer through sampling semantically adjacent augmented conditions. Given the negligible difference in transition probabilities, VK = {ck}K k=1 offers meaningful gradient signal for dense supervision and can be seamlessly incorporated into GRPO training. More discussion is provided in the supplementary material. 10 J. Bu et al.

sections.title: Implementation Details
sections.content: Datasets and Models. Following previous works [16, 43, 47], the HPD [41] dataset is employed as the prompt dataset. It comprises over 100K prompts for training and separate set of 400 prompts for evaluation. We adopt Flux.1dev [14] as the training backbone, an advanced open-source T2I flow model recognized for its superior visual quality. For the Condition Enhancer, we utilize two leading models from the Qwen series: Qwen3-VL-8B [1] is deployed as the online VLM enhancer, while Qwen3-8B [44] serves as the offline LLM enhancer. Further implementation details are provided in the supplementary material. Baselines. The compared methods encompass the vanilla Flux model [14], FlowGRPO [19], DanceGRPO [43], TempFlow-GRPO [11] and DiffusionNFT [46]. Evaluation Metrics. To comprehensively assess the effectiveness of MV-GRPO, diverse set of metrics is employed for evaluation: (i) Leading VLM-based Reward Models: HPS-v3 [21] and UnifiedReward-v1/v2 (UR-v1/v2) [39]; (ii) CLIP/BLIPbased Reward Models: HPS-v2 [41], CLIP [26] and ImageReward (IR) [42]. Sampling Details. Each SDE rollout is conducted with group size of = 12. The total number of sampling steps is set as = 16 for efficiency. The noise level throughout the sampling process is governed by the hyperparameter η, which 1t . To ensure fair comparison, all is fixed at 0.7 in the expression σt = η baseline methods adopt the identical configuration described above. Training Details. We build MV-GRPO upon Flow-GRPO-Fast [19], an efficient variant of Flow-GRPO [19]. The training steps are configured as {0, 2, 4, 6}. Following prior studies [43, 47], we train MV-GRPO under two experimental settings: (i) Single-Reward, where the model is fine-tuned using single stateof-the-art reward model, specifically either HPS-v3 or UnifiedReward-v2; (ii) Multi-Reward, in which HPS-v3 and CLIP are jointly utilized as reward signals to improve training robustness and prevent potential reward-hacking. Optimization Details. If not specified, all experiments in this section are conducted on 16NVIDIA H200 GPUs with the batch size setting to 1. We employ the AdamW optimizer, specifying learning rate of 2 106 and weight decay of 1 104. bfloat16 (bf16) mixed-precision training is adopted for efficiency. (cid:113) 4.2 Main Results Quantitative Evaluation. As presented in Tab. 1, MV-GRPO demonstrates consistent superiority under both single reward (HPS-v3 or UnifiedReward-v2) and multi-reward (HPS-v3 + CLIP) settings. Specifically, with online VLM condition enhancer, MV-GRPO achieves the best performance across most metrics, particularly excelling in HPS metrics, ImageReward, coherence (UR-v2-C), and style (UR-v2-S), while offline LLM enhancer yields the second-best results. This can be attributed to the VLM enhancers ability to generate tailored samplespecific posterior captions, which more precisely describe the generated images Multi-View GRPO Fig. 6: Reward Curves during Training. Our MV-GRPO outperforms baselines in both convergence speed and performance ceiling under various training settings. Table 1: Quantitative comparison of different methods. The best results are in bold, while the second-best result is underlined. UR-v2-A, UR-v2-C, and UR-v2-S denote the Alignment, Coherence, and Style dimensions of UnifiedReward-v2, respectively. Furthermore, combining HPS-v3 and CLIP yields notable improvements for both metrics, proving that integrating complementary signals (HPS-v3 for semantic quality, CLIP for text alignment) boosts overall generation. These results validate our dense multi-view mapping paradigm enables more comprehensive optimization and achieves superior performance. The reward curves for the VLM enhancer during training are illustrated in Fig. 6. Qualitative Comparison. As depicted in Fig. 7 and Fig. 8, MV-GRPO consistently outperforms its competitors in semantic alignment, visual fidelity, and structural coherence. In the room and tower cases  (Fig. 7)  , it renders fine indoor and architectural details with superior clarity. For the skater case, MVGRPO enhances the scenes tension by vividly synthesizing facial expressions and clothing wrinkles. Similarly, in the daffodil and cave examples  (Fig. 8)  , MV-GRPO enriches the compositions with intricate background elements such as furnitures, moons, starry skies, and floral details, significantly elevating the cinematic atmosphere and aesthetic appeal of the generated images. Finally, in the ski case, MV-GRPO not only generates detailed figures but also optimizes the lighting and composition to create more immersive and expansive snowcovered environment. More results are presented in the supplementary material. 12 J. Bu et al. Fig. 7: Qualitative Comparisons with Baselines on HPS-v3. Table 2: Comparison in denoiser NFE and iteration time (sec). Table 3: Compatibility with Other GRPO Frameworks. HPS-v3 is employed as the reward model. Method NFE Iteration Time (s) Method HPS-v3 UR-v2 (avg) HPS-v2 CLIP IR UR-v Flow-GRPO-Fast [19] 13 156 + Data Augmentation + MV-GRPO (Ours) 13 156.26 1931.15 191.95 DanceGRPO-Fast [43] 0.149 0.153 + MV-GRPO (LLM) + MV-GRPO (VLM) 0.154 3.387 3.411 3.418 0.330 0.334 0.342 0.375 1.108 3.555 3.561 0.370 1.172 0.368 1.187 3. 4.3 Additional Analysis Comparison in Latency. As shown in Tab. 2, our MV-GRPO introduces only modest overhead compared with its baseline method, nearly 10 less than that of applying an equal amount of data augmentation. Moreover, since MV-GRPO requires no sample regeneration, it matches the baseline in terms of the denoiser NFE, further demonstrating the efficiency of augmenting the condition space. Compatibility with Other GRPO Frameworks. Similar to Flow-GRPO [19], DanceGRPO [43] stands as foundational work in flow-based GRPO. As depicted in Tab. 3, we integrate MV-GRPO into DanceGRPO-Fast (DanceGRPO equipped with the few-step training of Flow-GRPO-Fast) and achieve remarkable performance improvements, highlighting its flexibility and versatility. Multi-View GRPO 13 Fig. 8: Qualitative Comparisons with Baselines on UnifiedReward-v2. 4.4 Ablation Study We conducted ablation studies on the online VLM enhancer setting trained with reward model HPS-v3. The results are presented in Tab. 4. Effects of Condition Number. The number of augmented conditions determines the density of the condition-data reward mapping. Empirically, larger facilitates more thorough exploration of intra-group relationships, leading to better optimization and superior performance. As can be observed in Tab. 4 (a), performance improves with but tends to saturate at higher values. Notably, even with = G/2 = 6, the model achieves competitive performance with = = 12. However, given that the overhead of condition augmentation is small, = = 12 is chosen to maximize the density of the reward mapping. Effects of Condition Diversity. The diversity of augmented conditions stems from two aspects: (i) each augmented condition is derived from distinct SDE sample, and (ii) diverse set of VLM prompts PVLM covering various descriptive perspectives is employed to query the VLM. We ablate (i) by generating all conditions from the same ODE sample, and (ii) by removing the multi-perspective prompt set PVLM. As shown in Tab. 4 (b), removing either component leads to 14 J. Bu et al. Fig. 9: Augmented Conditions. MV-GRPO generates diverse augmented conditions by leveraging variations among SDE samples and multi-view descriptive prompts. Table 4: Ablation experiments on MV-GRPO components and hyperparameters. Components (a) Condition Number (b) Condition Diversity (c) Enhancer Scale 3.238 3.235 3.233 3.229 0.149 0.152 0.154 0.155 w/o VK = 3 = 6 = Values & Choices HPS-v3 UR-v2-A UR-v2-C UR-v2-S HPS-v2 CLIP IR UR-v1 0.378 1.152 3.553 0.377 1.170 3.567 0.372 1.178 3.573 0.370 1.193 3.569 0.336 0.374 1.183 3.560 0.334 0.376 1.174 3.564 0.370 1.193 3.569 0.340 0.344 0.378 1.175 3.575 0.370 1.193 3.569 0.340 w/o SDE query w/o PVLM MV-GRPO Qwen3-VL-2B Qwen3-VL-8B 3.668 3.684 3.696 3.701 0.332 0.335 0.338 0.340 3.269 3.293 3.326 3. 0.153 0.152 0.155 3.692 3.687 3.701 3.304 3.295 3.320 3.233 3.237 3.229 0.153 0.155 3.317 3. 3.694 3.701 3.238 3.229 notable decline in performance. This confirms that both sample-level stochasticity and prompt-level semantic variety are crucial for constructing robust and diverse augmented condition space. Examples of the augmented conditions used during training are illustrated in Fig. 9. Effects of Enhancer Scale. We investigate the impact of the Condition Enhancers parameter scale by comparing the originally adopted Qwen3-VL-8B with its lightweight variant, Qwen3-VL-2B. As depicted in Tab. 4 (c), Qwen3VL-8B yields better overall performance on primary metrics such as HPS-v3, IR, and UR-v2-C/S. However, it is noteworthy that the 2B model delivers remarkably competitive results, even marginally surpassing its 8B variant on metrics like UR-v1 and HPS-v2. This indicates that the core advantage of MV-GRPO stems fundamentally from the dense multi-view evaluation mechanism itself, rather than merely relying on the capacity of the Condition Enhancer.

sections.title: 5 Conclusion
sections.content: In this work, we identify that standard flow-based GRPO relies on sparse, single-view reward evaluation scheme that causes insufficient exploration of intragroup relationships and suboptimal performance. To this end, we introduce MV-GRPO, novel reinforcement learning framework that shifts the alignment paradigm from single-view to dense, multi-view supervision. MV-GRPO leverages flexible Condition Enhancer module to augment the condition space with semantically adjacent yet diverse descriptors, enabling dense multi-view reward mapping that captures rich semantic attributes and provides comprehensive advantage estimation, without the overhead of sample regeneration. Experiments demonstrate MV-GRPOs superiority over existing state-of-the-art methods. Multi-View GRPO

From Sparse to Dense: Multi-View GRPO for Flow Models via Augmented Condition Space Supplementary Material

sections.title: A Overview
sections.content: In the supplementary material, we present additional implementation details (Section B), additional qualitative results (Section C), all text prompts used in image generation (Section D), more discussion on condition enhancement (Section E), the limitations of our method (Section F), the ethical statement (Section G), the reproducibility statement (Section H), as well as the declaration on LLM usage (Section I), as supplement to the main paper.

sections.title: B Additional Implementation Details
sections.content: B.1 Hyperparameter Configuration Tab. 5 lists the specific hyperparameter settings employed in our study. These parameters were maintained consistently across all our experiments. Table 5: Hyperparameter settings in our experiments. Parameter Value Parameter Random seed Train batch size Warmup steps Dataloader workers Eta Group size Sampling steps Init same noise The number of GPUs 42 Learning rate 1 Weight decay 0 Mixed precision 4 Max grad norm 0.7 Sampler seed 12 Scheduler shift 16 Adv. clip max Yes Condition Number 16 Clip range Value 2 106 1 104 bfloat16 1.0 1223627 3 5.0 12 1 104 B.2 Prompts for VLM and LLM Condition Enhancer VLM Prompts. The VLM prompt for MV-GRPO consists of two components: an instruction set PVLM containing diverse descriptive perspectives, and prompt template. During each VLM query, specific instruction PVLM is randomly sampled from PVLM and inserted into the template to interact with the VLM Condition Enhancer. The content of PVLM is presented in Tab. 6, while the VLM prompt template is illustrated in Fig. 10. Multi-View GRPO 19 Fig. 10: VLM Prompt Template. It integrates descriptive instruction from PVLM, along with an image and its original text prompt, to construct complete VLM prompt for querying the VLM Condition Enhancer to obtain augmented conditions. Table 6: List of instructions in the set PVLM used to query the VLM. Each instruction guides the model to focus on specific visual dimension when generating conditions. ID Instructions Focus on the main subjects and key attributes. Focus on actions, motion, or interactions. Focus on scene layout and spatial relationships. Focus on lighting conditions and shadows. Focus on color palette and contrast. Focus on camera angle, lens feel, and depth of field. 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 Focus on background/setting and environmental context. 8 9 Focus on textures and material details. Focus on mood, atmosphere, and tone. 20 J. Bu et al. LLM Prompts. Similar to the VLM enhancer setting, the LLM prompt for MV-GRPO also features two components: an instruction set PLLM containing three operations (addition, deletion, and rewriting), and prompt template. For each LLM query, an operation PLLM is randomly selected from PLLM and incorporated into the template to facilitate interaction with the LLM Condition Enhancer. The details of PLLM are listed in Tab. 7, and the LLM prompt template is depicted in Fig. 11. Fig. 11: LLM Prompt Template. This template incorporates specific operation from PLLM and memory buffer of prior outputs, guiding the LLM Condition Enhancer to refine the original text prompt into diverse augmented conditions. Table 7: List of operations in the set PLLM used to query the LLM. Each operation directs the model to modify the input prompt while maintaining semantic consistency. Multi-View GRPO 21 ID Instructions 1 ADD: Expand the prompt by enriching existing elements with descriptive details (<= 20 tokens), strictly avoiding the introduction of new subjects. 2 DELETE: Remove some non-essential details (<= 20 tokens) to produce concise caption, but the output must remain valid description of image. PARAPHRASE: Swap words/phrases for close synonyms (adjectives -> near-synonyms; nouns -> another common term for the same thing).

sections.title: C Additional Qualitative Results
sections.content: In this section, we present more qualitative comparisons between the proposed MV-GRPO and existing flow-based GRPO methods in Fig. 13, Fig. 14, Fig. 15 and Fig. 16, additional visual results of MV-GRPO in Fig. 17, Fig. 18, Fig. 19 and Fig. 20, along with generated results using the same prompts and different random seeds (0/1/2) in Fig. 21 and Fig. 22.

sections.title: D Text Prompts for Image Generation
sections.content: All prompts used to generate images in this paper are listed in Tab. 8 and Tab. 9.

sections.title: E More Discussion on Condition Enhancement
sections.content: In this section, we provide deeper analysis on condition enhancement for flowbased GRPO. Specifically, we rationalize the validity of using augmented conditions to optimize original samples through the lens of Equivalent SDE Noise. Standard Flow SDE. First, we revisit the standard flow SDE widely adopted in existing GRPO frameworks [19,43]. As illustrated in Fig. 12 (a), given noisy sample xt and condition c, the flow model first executes deterministic ODE sampling to estimate the underlying noise-free sample x0t and the corresponding Gaussian noise x1t at the current timestep t: x0t = xt vθ(xt, t, c), x1t = xt + (1 t) vθ(xt, t, c), (18) (19) where vθ(xt, t, c) denotes the predicted flow velocity given condition c. Subsequently, x0t and x1t are combined to yield the transition mean µθ(xt, c) of the current SDE rollout: µθ(xt, c) = (1 t)x0t + + + (cid:18) (cid:19) σ2 2t x1t, (20) 22 J. Bu et al. Fig. 12: Illustration of Equivalent SDE Noise. (a) Standard flow SDE generates samples based on the transition mean anchored to single condition c. (b) When substituting with an augmented condition c, the transition mean shifts accordingly. To reach the original samples, an equivalent noise term ϵSDE is implicitly required. in the figure are shorthand for µθ(xt, c) and µθ(xt, c), respectively. The µθ and µ θ in which stands for the step size. Finally, each SDE sample xSDE by injecting randomly sampled Gaussian noise ϵSDE into µθ(xt, c): t1 is obtained xSDE t1 = µθ(xt, c) + σt tϵSDE. (21) t1 , xSDE2 t1 In Fig. 12 (a), we denote the specific Gaussian noise components sampled during independent SDE rollouts as ϵSDE1, ϵSDE2 and ϵSDE3, which consequently lead to distinct SDE samples xSDE1 Condition Enhancement. As illustrated in Fig. 12 (b), the Condition Enhancement of MV-GRPO initiates the process from the identical noisy latent state xt but substitutes the input condition for the flow model with an augmented view c. Given the shift in flow velocity (from vθ(xt, t, c) to vθ(xt, t, c)), the deterministic estimates for the clean sample and the Gaussian noise will both undergo corresponding transformations: t1 , respectively. and xSDE3 x0t 0t, x1t 1t. Therefore, the transition mean also changes accordingly: µθ(xt, c) = (1 t)x 0t + (cid:18) + + (cid:19) σ2 2t 1t. (22) (23) If we expect the updated mean µθ(xt, c) to still transition to the original SDE t1 , an equivalent noise term ϵSDE satisfying the following relationship state xSDE should be explicitly sampled: t1 = µθ(xt, c) + σt xSDE tϵSDE. (24) Multi-View GRPO 23 Therefore, utilizing the augmented condition to optimize the original SDE sample xSDE t1 fundamentally hinges on the proximity between the probability of sampling the original noise ϵSDE and that of the equivalent noise ϵSDE. Since measuring the sampling probability discrepancy between stochastic noise terms is mathematically equivalent to quantifying the divergence between the two probability densities pθ(xt1xt, c) and pθ(xt1xt, c) (as we discussed in the main paper), this probability gap corresponds directly to the probability drift δ(c, c) defined in Eq. 17 of the main text, which remains minimal for the vast majority of samples (see Fig. 5 in the main paper). This can be interpreted as follows: given the semantic similarity between and c, the discrepancy between their induced transition means µθ(xt, c) and µθ(xt, c) is sufficiently small. Consequently, the SDE noise required to reach the same target state xSDE t1 remains proximate, resulting in minimal difference in sampling probability.

sections.title: F Limitation
sections.content: Despite the advancements of MV-GRPO in preference alignment for flow models, it faces certain constraints. First, its effectiveness may be limited in tasks with rigid or predefined conditioning signals (e.g., class-conditional generation on specific dataset), where meaningful condition enhancements are difficult to formulate. Furthermore, the quality of augmented conditions is inherently bounded by the visual understanding and reasoning capabilities of current VLMs or LLMs. However, we anticipate that this limitation will be naturally mitigated as the performance of these models continues to advance.

sections.title: G Ethical Statement
sections.content: We are committed to maintaining the ethical standards and fostering responsible innovation throughout this research. To the best of our knowledge, our study, including the data, methodologies, and applications involved, does not present any ethical concerns. All experiments were conducted in strict accordance with established ethical frameworks, ensuring the integrity, transparency, and reliability of our research, with careful attention to responsible use.

sections.title: H Reproducibility Statement
sections.content: To ensure full reproducibility and support the broader research community, we will publicly release the source code of MV-GRPO. We envision these resources serving as valuable baseline for future research in flow-based GRPO, facilitating further innovation and progress in the field.
